claim: The glyph positional constraints (word-initial set {o,c,q,s,d}, word-final set {y,n,l,r,o}) are produced by a VigenÃ¨re-style polyalphabetic cipher where the key position within a word determines the allowable glyph set, such that position 1 maps to a restricted alphabet and the final position maps to a different restricted alphabet, compressing the effective entropy at word boundaries.
evidence_for: Positional glyph constraints are too regular to be grammatical â€” they hold across all sections and both Currier variants
Belief scores 0.77â€“0.87 confirm that positional constraints are cipher-structural, not grammatical
A VigenÃ¨re-style key cycling over word length would naturally produce position-dependent glyph restrictions if certain key values map to restricted sets
evidence_against: Classical VigenÃ¨re on a 25-glyph alphabet with a short key would produce periodic index-of-coincidence peaks that have not been confirmed
If the key length equals word length, it collapses to a running-key cipher, which would produce near-uniform positional distributions rather than constrained ones
Average word length of 5.03 suggests short words dominate; VigenÃ¨re period detection requires longer sequences
test: Compute index of coincidence (IC) for each word-position slice (position 1, 2, 3, 4, 5+) across the full corpus; test for periodic structure in IC values as a function of position offset; apply Kasiski test to sequences of same-position glyphs extracted from all words of the same length.
test_metric: Per-position IC values; Kasiski key-length estimate from same-position glyph sequences; chi-square test of glyph uniformity at each word position
test_threshold: Per-position IC significantly above random (IC > 0.065 for a 25-glyph alphabet) AND Kasiski suggesting key length 3â€“7 would support polyalphabetic hypothesis; IC near 1/25 = 0.04 at non-boundary positions would refute it.

reasoning: Polyalphabetic cipher would explain positional constraints without requiring them to be grammatical, and would imply a specific key-length that could be estimated â€” dramatically narrowing the cipher search space if confirmed.
